### Post by kaigray on 2007-11-09
After at LOT of time spent attempting to get an external monitor to work  using BigScreen I was finally successful thanks to many of those here that posted.  

I now have my screen extended from my ThinkPad T60 on to my external Dell E198.  The resolution of both screens is now correct (the T60 is 1400x1050@60, the Dell is 1440@900@60) and life is grand. I hope this helps.

Here are my specs, what I did and my xorg.conf:

[LIST]
[*]IBM Thinkpad T60
[*]ATI X1400 Graphics Card
[*]Dell E198WFp External Monitor
[*]Ubuntu 7.10 (Gutsy)
[*]ATI driver 8.42.3 (Drivers [here]("https://a248.e.akamai.net/f/674/9206/0/www2.ati.com/drivers/linux/ati-driver-installer-8.42.3-x86.x86_64.run"))
[/LIST]

Display Resolutions:
[LIST]
[*]ThinkPad: 1400x1050@60
[*]Dell E198: 1440x900260
[/LIST]

I wanted BigScreen to expand my screen to the right.  My thinkPad screen acts as my main screen and the Dell monitor sits to its right.

I was unable to get Compiz working properly so I compromised.  I'd rather have the additional screen space then the eye candy.  

I used the flgrx driver as it seemed to be the only thing to work properly.

Here is my working xorg.conf: (thanks to redwheelbarrow and others)

```

# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the /etc/X11/xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man /etc/X11/xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg
#
#   ********************************
#   ** BIG DESKTOP CONFIGURATION  **
#   ********************************
#
#  redwheelbarrow 03-10-07

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier     "Default Layout"
	Screen      0  "aticonfig-Screen[0]" 0 0
	InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
	InputDevice    "stylus" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice    "cursor" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice    "eraser" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice    "Synaptics Touchpad"
EndSection

Section "Files"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/X11/fonts/cyrillic"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi/:unscaled"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/X11/fonts/Type1"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/fonts/X11/misc"
	FontPath     "/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load  "i2c"
	Load  "bitmap"
	Load  "ddc"
	Load  "dri"
	Load  "extmod"
	Load  "freetype"
	Load  "glx"
	Load  "int10"
	Load  "type1"
	Load  "vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Generic Keyboard"
	Driver      "kbd"
	Option	    "CoreKeyboard"
	Option	    "XkbRules" "xorg"
	Option	    "XkbModel" "pc105"
	Option	    "XkbLayout" "us"
	Option	    "XkbOptions" "lv3:ralt_switch"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Configured Mouse"
	Driver      "mouse"
	Option	    "CorePointer"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
	Option	    "Protocol" "ExplorerPS/2"
	Option	    "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
	Option	    "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Synaptics Touchpad"
	Driver      "synaptics"
	Option	    "SendCoreEvents" "true"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/psaux"
	Option	    "Protocol" "auto-dev"
	Option	    "HorizScrollDelta" "0"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "stylus"
	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/wacom"
	Option	    "Type" "stylus"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "eraser"
	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/wacom"
	Option	    "Type" "eraser"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "cursor"
	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/wacom"
	Option	    "Type" "cursor"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier   "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
	Option	    "VendorName" "ATI Proprietary Driver"
	Option	    "ModelName" "Generic Autodetecting Monitor"
	Option	    "DPMS" "true"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier  "aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Driver      "fglrx"
	Option	    "DesktopSetup" "horizontal"
	Option	    "VideoOverlay" "on"
	Option	    "OpenGLOverlay" "off"
	Option	    "EnablePrivateBackZ" "yes"
	Option	    "HSync2" "83"
	Option	    "VRefresh2" "75"
	Option "DesktopSetup" "LVDS,AUTO"
	Option 	    "Mode2" "1440x900" #Resolution for second monitor
	BusID       "PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier "aticonfig-Screen[0]"
	Device     "aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Monitor    "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
	DefaultDepth     24
	SubSection "Display"
		Viewport   0 0
		Depth     24
		Virtual 1440 1050
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "DRI"
	Mode         0666
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
	Option	    "Composite" "Disable"
EndSection


```

---

### Post by chocolateboy on 2007-11-10
Here's something to try if you're still stuck in clone mode (despite setting everything up correctly in xorg.conf), or if the settings work for gdm but not for gnome.

Background: I couldn't get this to work with a TV as the secondary monitor. The display was correct on the gdm screen (I could move the pointer over to the TV), but after logging in, the display would revert to clone mode. The following (via [this site]("http://tinyurl.com/29auy5")) fixed it:

[LIST=1]
[*]Follow the relevant instructions in this thread to get it almost working - specifically, gdm should display in big desktop mode
[*]Login and select System -> Preferences -> Screen Resolution
[*]Select the appropriate big desktop resolution i.e. the resolution of the whole desktop - in my case both of my screens have a resolution of 1024 x 768, so I selected 2048 x 768
[/LIST]

---

### Post by drowd on 2007-11-15
So after following this thread and many others, I finally have two xorg.conf's that I am mostly ok with.  One of them was generated by default when I installed gutsy.  It seems to be a fairly messy config file, but it does the job.  It does all the Compiz stuff just fine; however, it is currently cloning my monitors (2 x HP LP2065).  Over the last several days of trying to get everything working, I decided that I needed to completely rewrite my xorg.conf because it seemed like there was a lot of junk in there.  with the help of this thread and some of the other guides, I was able to get my desktop to span across both monitors AND keep Compiz AND rewrite my xorg.conf (which was a huge learning experience for me, but fun too).

However, I have now come up against the wall that it seems like numerous folks have run into with running slightly older video cards.  I'm not exactly sure what the cause is (graphics card memory? old hardware?), but I have read about the symptoms.  I'm running an ATi X800, and when running Compiz + BigDesktop, I hit a 2048x768 maximum resolution for both monitors combined.  Again, my understanding is that this is due to a 2048x2048 limitation of the video card.

So, while I learned a lot and worked hard to get everything up and running, that resolution will not be one that is an acceptable working environment.  So I wanted to examine my alternatives and see what the experts thought :D  Also, most of these solutions are ones that I have just read about briefly, but I have been unable to find a definitive guide.

The first option I had heard about involved aligning your BigDeskop's vertically rather than horizontally.  This would not be the _best_ solution, but I could certainly get used to it and it would be at a more than acceptable resolution to this issue.  As I understand it, instead of your virtual resolution (not sure if that is the correct term) being 2048x768, it would be 1280x2048.  Even though I'm used to running 1600x1200 on both of these guys, i can certainly work with 1280x1024.

The second option I read about involved having two independent cubes on each desktop.  I'm not even sure exactly how it worked.  But from what I gathered, Compiz was somehow invoked in two different instances, one for each monitor.  It also seemed like it required a good bit of surgery on your xorg.conf.  However, after noting the similarities in the "Xinerama HOWTO" thread, its possible this "two independent invocations" method that I read about was for Xinerama users only.

Finally, are there any alternatives that I missed (other than upgrading video card or not running compiz) to the 2048x768 problem?  Would one of the other modes like Xinerama or MergedFB be better?

And here is a copy of my xorg.conf (the one that i rewrote to work with BigDesktop)

```

# xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier     "Default Layout"
	Screen      0  "Default Screen" 0 0
	InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
EndSection

Section "Files"
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load  "glx"
	Load  "dbe"
	Load  "v4l"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Generic Keyboard"
	Driver      "kbd"
	Option	    "CoreKeyboard"
	Option	    "XkbRules" "xorg"
	Option	    "XkbModel" "pc105"
	Option	    "XkbLayout" "us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Configured Mouse"
	Driver      "mouse"
	Option	    "CorePointer"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
	Option	    "Protocol" "ImPS/2"
	Option	    "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
	Option	    "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier   "HP LP2065"
	VendorName   "Hewlett-Packard"
	ModelName    "HP LP2065 Flat Panel Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier  "ATI Technologies Inc R430 [Radeon X800 XL] (PCIe)"
	Driver      "fglrx"
	BoardName   "ati"
	Option	    "DesktopSetup" "horizontal"
	Option	    "ForceMonitors" "crt1,tmds1"
	Option	    "HSync2" "30-92"
	Option	    "VRefresh2" "48-85"
	BusID       "PCI:64:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier "Default Screen"
	Device     "ATI Technologies Inc R430 [Radeon X800 XL] (PCIe)"
	Monitor    "HP LP2065"
	DefaultDepth     24
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
	Option	    "Composite" "0"
EndSection

```

### Post by kickehy on 2007-12-06
Well, I did an upgrade originally and I think that royally screwed me up.....so after many frustrating hours (and after my xrandr wouldn't work anyways) I just backed up everything and did a fresh install. I'm pretty sure that fixed most of my problems. Here's my xorg.conf and maybe it'll help you:

```

Section "ServerLayout"
        Identifier     "Default Layout"
        Screen      0  "aticonfig-Screen[0]" 0 0
        InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
        InputDevice     "Logitech MX1000" "CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "Files"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
        Identifier  "Generic Keyboard"
        Driver      "kbd"
        Option      "CoreKeyboard"
        Option      "XkbRules" "xorg"
        Option      "XkbModel" "pc105"
        Option      "XkbLayout" "us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
        Identifier      "Logitech MX1000"
        Driver          "evdev"
        Option          "Name"          "Logitech USB RECEIVER"
        Option          "HWHEELRelativeAxisButtons" "7 6 8 9"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
        Identifier   "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
        Option      "VendorName" "ATI Proprietary Driver"
        Option      "ModelName" "Generic Autodetecting Monitor"
        Option      "DPMS" "true"
EndSection

Section "Device"
        Identifier  "aticonfig-Device[0]"
        Driver      "fglrx"
        Option      "VideoOverlay" "on"
        Option      "OpenGLOverlay" "off"
        Option      "DesktopSetup" "horizontal,reverse" #Enable Big Desktop
        Option      "Mode2" "1280x1024" #Resolution for second monitor
        Option      "PairMode" "1280x1024+1024x768" # Pair mode resolutions
        Option      "HSync2" "82" #This sets the horizontal sync for the secondary display.
        Option      "VRefresh2" "75" #This sets the refresh rate of the secondary display.
EndSection

Section "Screen"
        Identifier "aticonfig-Screen[0]"
        Device     "aticonfig-Device[0]"
        Monitor    "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
        DefaultDepth     24
        SubSection "Display"
                Viewport   0 0
                Depth     24
        EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "DRI"
        Mode         0666
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
        Option      "Composite" "Disable"
EndSection


```

The only problem I have now is that there is like 80 pixels that I can't see on the right side and on the smaller 1024x768 monitor there is 256 pixels on the bottom that I can't see. Even though there are those pixels...I can still maximize a window with no problem on each screen. If anyone has any ideas how to correct the screens that's cool...but at least I have dual screens now. :) Hope this helps you kobkob. If you need anything else let me know....not too sure how much help I'll be.

### Post by evernight on 2007-12-09
First off, great thread.  I apologize if I didn't read far enough to find somebody with exactly my problem.  I'm running Daryna (Mint/Gutsy 2.6.22) Gnome and trying to configure dual monitor support on an integrated ATI X1250.  I've got the "restricted" driver up with no problem on my CRT from the VGA port.  Tricky part is, my other monitor is a Sony WEGA running in 1920x1080i.  It's that "i" part that I think is causing the most trouble.

Previously, I had the DVI-to-HDMI running via ATI Catalyst.  Due to the overscan, I used 1776x1000 as my resolution with a 30 refresh.  I'd like to try something like that under Mint.

So far, I've confirmed that the DVI is at least talking to my TV, as the "Video 7" box up in the corner flickers in time with my CRT on boot.  I'm not sure if "aticonfig --initial dual-head" has ever had any real effect.  I followed Ziox's original instructions.  When I do "sudo aticonfig --query-monitor" I get:

```

  Connected monitors: crt1, tmds2i
  Enabled monitors: crt1, tmds2i

```

...which looks funny to me because I didn't expect tmds2i, which I *assume* is my TV, to show up as enabled.  I tried "sudo aticonfig --enable-monitor=crt1,tmds2i" and lost the picture on CRT, which makes me wonder if my assumption was wrong in the first place.  If so, there's one reason my TV wouldn't recognize the signal, as it can't display above a refresh rate of 30Hz.  There could be plenty other reasons, naturally.

One thing I observed is that the GUI tool in Mint didn't list an option for a second monitor.  I'm not sure what how that's populated.  Intuitively, I'd expect it to read xorg.conf, but I don't know.

Is there any further test I should do to make sure Mint is recognizing the DVI head?  Has anybody successfully dual-screened to an interlaced LCD?

Here's the tail portion of my xorg.conf as running right now:
```
Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Generic Video Card"
	Driver		"fglrx"
	Busid		"PCI:1:5:0"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"TPV D1770"
	Option		"DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Device		"Generic Video Card"
	Monitor		"TPV D1770"
	Defaultdepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Modes		"1280x1024"	"1024x768"	"800x600"	"640x480"
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
  screen "Default Screen"
	Inputdevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	Inputdevice	"Configured Mouse"
	
	# Uncomment if you have a wacom tablet
	#	InputDevice     "stylus"	"SendCoreEvents"
	#	InputDevice     "cursor"	"SendCoreEvents"
	#	InputDevice     "eraser"	"SendCoreEvents"
EndSection
Section "Module"
	Load		"glx"
EndSection
Section "Extensions"
	Option		"Composite"	"0"
EndSection
```

Any help would be much appreciated.

](*,)

### Post by kobkob on 2007-12-10
Hey All,

I've gotten closer.  I restored my xorg.conf file.  Then I stripped all the crap out of it... and there was a lot of crap.  Then I started over.  This time I skipped the part about using the aticonfig tool and just cut and paste the lines for the device section.  When I restarted X I got a couple errors in the gdm log.  By commenting out a couple lines in my xorf.conf file that were obsolete I was able to get a clean restart of X.

Now the two screens come up.  One has the login screen and the other just a solid color.  I can move the mouse around either screen/monitor.  The screen real estate and the orientation seems correct.  However, when I login the screens flash black and after about 30 seconds to a minute I am returned to the login screen.

Any ideas?  Need more info to diagnose?  I'll play with it more tomorrow night and check out the gdm logs and see if can figure out what is crashing.

Cheers.

### Post by Mcavity on 2007-12-12

### Post by sboddy on 2007-12-17
Hi all,

I don't know about everyone else, but I very frequently switch between Single and Dual screens with my laptop. Think twice a day: morning at work, and evening at home. A long time ago someone posted some aliases (DSon and DSoff) which were helpful, but still a bit of a drag to switch to the console and run each time.

So, here's the new and improved version.

What it does:
It figures out what configuration you currently have (single/dual), then it checks how many monitors are connected (1/more). If they differ then it executes the appropriate command from the earlier DSon/off aliases.

There's some funky stuff to get access to the display for running aticonfig against, which I ripped from the atieventsd script from /etc/init.d/

All this is wrapped in a nice init script.

Instructions:
Attached is a gzip'd script. Download it, uncompress it, check it out (look here for a followup "OK" from more experienced folk if you don't understand what it does), copy it to /etc/init.d making sure to make it owned by root and executable.

Now as root run the command: update-rc.d ati-dual-detect defaults

This will add it as something to be run at bootup, after gdm has started.

Now, when you boot up, it checks for a second connected display, and if necessary bounce gdm so it comes up in the correct config.

Hope someone finds it useful, and thanks to everyone else who's posted (esp. Ziox)

---

Now, when you boot up, it checks for a second connected display, and if necessary bounce gdm so it comes up in the correct config.

(**) Option "Protocol" "standard"
(**) Generic Keyboard: Protocol: standard
(**) Option "AutoRepeat" "500 30"

My video card is a ATI Technologies Inc RV370 5B64 FireGL V3100 
I'm using the fglrx driver
This is for Ubuntu 7.10
Thanks!!!

### Post by technel on 2008-01-01
I followed all of Ziox's tutorials TWICE to no avail. I literally was about to put the monitor back into the box and bring it back when I decided to try one last time--this time with the manual configuration.

When I used the ATI config tool, the monitor would go black with a very large mouse icon stuck in the upper left hand corner. I followed the instructions on the manual xorg.conf config in this thread and it worked beautifully.

I can hardly overstate how happy I am that it worked! But just be sure to **try both** if the ati-config doesn't work.

---

### Post by ilyasyildirim on 2008-01-04
Hi Ubuntu Users,

I am writing here to share my experience with setting up a 2960x1050 resolution xorg.conf file in Ubuntu 7.10. 

I changed my OS to Ubuntu 7.10 from Suse Linux 10.1 in the hope that setting up a dual monitor system would be easy, but I was wrong. Setting up an xorg.conf in linux is WAAAAYYY hard, let me first tell this!

My system is a Dell Dimension 4700 with Radeon X300SE graphics card. I have 2 monitors; Acer AL2216W 22" wide screen and DELL E173FP 17" flat panel. Acer supports max resolution of 1680x1050 while DELL E173FP supports 1280x1024. So, I wanted to setup an xorg.conf that will support 2960x1050 resolution.

Let me tell you that you will have to REBOOT the machine often in order the computer to understand the xorg.conf file. So, it is better to make a backup of a working xorg.conf file. (Trust me, you will need this file sooo many times!)

1. You have to go to the Restricted Driver Manager in System > Administrator, and use that driver for this graphics card (I did not do anything fancy on this part. After installing Ubuntu 7.10, I have chosen this card for my graphics card). REBOOT!

After rebooting, the machine will use this particular driver when u change the xorg.conf file (specifically the driver 'fglrx'). Without rebooting, you will not be able to use this driver or the command 'aticonfig'.

2. Then, I changed the xorg.conf file with the following: (This is for my system, so you need to change the display resolutions)
=====================================================

# xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "ServerLayout"

	# Uncomment if you have a wacom tablet
	#	InputDevice     "stylus"	"SendCoreEvents"
	#	InputDevice     "cursor"	"SendCoreEvents"
	#	InputDevice     "eraser"	"SendCoreEvents"
	Identifier     "Default Layout"
	Screen         "Default Screen" 0 0
	InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
EndSection

Section "Files"
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load  "glx"
        Load            "GLcore"
        Load            "dri"
        Load            "v4l"

	Load	"i2c"
	Load	"bitmap"
	Load	"ddc"
	Load	"extmod"
	Load	"freetype"
	Load	"int10"
	Load	"vbe"

EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Generic Keyboard"
	Driver      "kbd"
	Option	    "CoreKeyboard"
	Option	    "XkbRules" "xorg"
	Option	    "XkbModel" "pc101"
	Option	    "XkbLayout" "us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Configured Mouse"
	Driver      "mouse"
	Option	    "CorePointer"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
	Option	    "Protocol" "ImPS/2"
	Option	    "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
	Option	    "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "stylus"
	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
	Option	    "Type" "stylus"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "eraser"
	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
	Option	    "Type" "eraser"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "cursor"
	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
	Option	    "Type" "cursor"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
        VendorName "ACR"
        ModelName "Acer AL2216W"
	Identifier   "Acer AL2216W"
	HorizSync    30.0 - 82.0
	VertRefresh  56.0 - 76.0
        Mode    "1680x1050"     # vfreq 59.954Hz, hfreq 65.290kHz
               DotClock        146.250000 
               HTimings        1680 1784 1960 2240
               VTimings        1050 1053 1059 1089
               Flags   "+HSync" "-VSync"
        EndMode
	Option	    "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier  	"ATI Technologies Inc RV370 5B60 [Radeon X300 (PCIE)]"
	Driver      	"fglrx"
	Option 		"DesktopSetup"  "horizontal" 	#Enable Big Desktop
	Option 		"Mode2" "1280x1024" 		#Resolution for second monitor
	Option 		"EnablePrivateBackZ" "yes" 	#Enable 3d support <= May Not Work
	Option 		"HSync2" "64" 		#This sets the horizontal sync for the secondary display. 
	Option 		"VRefresh2" "60" 		#This sets the refresh rate of the secondary display.
	Option 		"DesktopSetup" "LVDS,AUTO" 	#the types of monitors that is connected LVDS = LCD, CRT, AUTO
	BusID       	"PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier "Default Screen"
	Device     "ATI Technologies Inc RV370 5B60 [Radeon X300 (PCIE)]"
	Monitor    "Acer AL2216W"
	DefaultDepth     24
	SubSection "Display"
		Modes    "1680x1050" "1280x1024" "1280x960" "1280x800" "1152x864" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
	Option	    "Composite" "0"
EndSection
===================================================

If you have the same machine I have (DELL Dimension 4700 with ATI Radeon X300SE card), the above xorg.conf should work for you (assuming that you make the necessary changes on the Monitor and Screen).

3. Now, the next point was really hard. The above xorg.conf file works ok, except that the screen size is set to 3360x1050. As a result, when you move the mouse on the second monitor and come to the right edge, it will still move to right even though you dont see the mouse cursor on the screen. This was annoying. Also, when you put a wallpaper, computer things that the resolution is 3360x1050 and adjusts things accordingly. But you see the picture clipped on the second monitor. So, even though it is not the perfect solution, changing from 3360x1050 to 2960x1050 will improve the desktop image. But doing this is sooo tricky; I am not sure if this procedure will work in 3-4 months (things change, you know). 

4. Then I followed some of the protocols of kEinstein (I hope I wrote his nick right). But his methodology is very complicated (at least for me). I have been struggling for a week to setup this dual monitor system, but all the time some weird things happens. In any case, at this point, I gave the following command:

aticonfig   --add-pairmode=1680x1050+1280x1050

(I did not restart or reboot the machine after this step)

5. Then, I changed the xorg.conf file as follows:

================================================
# xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "ServerLayout"

	# Uncomment if you have a wacom tablet
	#	InputDevice     "stylus"	"SendCoreEvents"
	#	InputDevice     "cursor"	"SendCoreEvents"
	#	InputDevice     "eraser"	"SendCoreEvents"
	Identifier     "Default Layout"
	Screen         "Default Screen" 0 0
	InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
EndSection

Section "Files"
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load  "glx"
	Load  "GLcore"
	Load  "dri"
	Load  "v4l"
	Load  "i2c"
	Load  "bitmap"
	Load  "ddc"
	Load  "extmod"
	Load  "freetype"
	Load  "int10"
	Load  "vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Generic Keyboard"
	Driver      "kbd"
	Option	    "CoreKeyboard"
	Option	    "XkbRules" "xorg"
	Option	    "XkbModel" "pc101"
	Option	    "XkbLayout" "us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Configured Mouse"
	Driver      "mouse"
	Option	    "CorePointer"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
	Option	    "Protocol" "ImPS/2"
	Option	    "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
	Option	    "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "stylus"
	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
	Option	    "Type" "stylus"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "eraser"
	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
	Option	    "Type" "eraser"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "cursor"
	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
	Option	    "Type" "cursor"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier   "Acer AL2216W"
	VendorName   "ACR"
	ModelName    "Acer AL2216W"
	HorizSync    30.0 - 82.0
	VertRefresh  56.0 - 76.0
	Option	    "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"

#	Option 		"Mode2" "1280x1024" 		#Resolution for second monitor
#	Option	    	"PairModes"        "1680x1050+1280x1050"  
	Identifier  "ATI Technologies Inc RV370 5B60 [Radeon X300 (PCIE)]"
	Driver      "fglrx"
	Option	    "DesktopSetup" "horizontal" 	#Enable Big Desktop
	Option	    "EnablePrivateBackZ" "no" 	#Enable 3d support <= May Not Work
	Option	    "HSync2" "64" 		#This sets the horizontal sync for the secondary display. 
	Option	    "VRefresh2" "60" 		#This sets the refresh rate of the secondary display.
	Option	    "PairModes" "1680x1050+1680x1050"
	BusID       "PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier "Default Screen"
	Device     "ATI Technologies Inc RV370 5B60 [Radeon X300 (PCIE)]"
	Monitor    "Acer AL2216W"
	DefaultDepth     24
	SubSection "Display"
		Viewport   0 0
		Depth     24
		Modes    "1680x1050" "1280x1050"
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "DRI"
	Mode         0666
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
	Option	    "Composite" "0"
EndSection
==============================================

After this, I rebooted the machine. The machine is really strange; now it started to see the resolution as 2960x1050!!! This is purely luck! If I wanted to understand the logic behind 'pairmodes', I would assume to put "1680x1050+1280x1050" (which I have done a lot lot times without getting a result).  

So, this worked for me. Also, I was most of the time using 1280x1024 because the max. resolution of DELL is 1280x1024. But it looks like that it does not work on setting things up in linux when u enter this resolution. I will suggest you to use 1280x1050.

Hope somebody can make use of this. And last suggestion, when you see something working, DONT do anything else!!! :)

Good luck

---

### Post by ilyasyildirim on 2008-01-15
I just wanted to tell you guys that the latest version of ati catalysis (1.7) has problems in it if you want to combine 2 different resolutions with --add-pairmode. I have been struggling with this for a long time. Installing the previous version (1.6 - driver ver 8.43.2) does not have this problem. Using the ATI Catalysis center, it is easy to play with the monitors. --add-pairmode works in this version; so you can add 1680x1050+1280x1024 resolution to the system. Ver. 1.7 does not let you do it. Hope this can help some of you.

---

# xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)

	Identifier	"Main Screen"

	SubSection "Display"
		Modes		"1280x1024"	"1280x960"	"1152x864"	"1024x768"	"832x624"	"800x600"	"720x400"	"640x480"

	Identifier	"Second Screen"
	Device		"1 Generic Video Card"
	Monitor		"F-419"
	Defaultdepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Modes		"1280x1024"	"1280x960"	"1152x864"	"1024x768"	"832x624"	"800x600"	"720x400"	"640x480"
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
	Screen        0   "Main Screen"
    	Screen       1   "Second Screen" RightOf "Main Screen"
	Inputdevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	Inputdevice	"Configured Mouse"
	
	# Uncomment if you have a wacom tablet
	#	InputDevice     "stylus"	"SendCoreEvents"
	#	InputDevice     "cursor"	"SendCoreEvents"
	#	InputDevice     "eraser"	"SendCoreEvents"
	Option "Xinerama" "true"
EndSection
Section "Module"
	Load		"glx"
EndSection
Section "Extensions"
	Option		"Composite"	"0"
EndSection

What to do?

### Post by frogotronic on 2008-02-06
> **kEinstein said:**
> Hi there! I'm sorry for the long absence in writing. Unfortunately I had to prepare for my last exam - but now that it's over, I had some time to spend on xorg.conf, dualhead with different resolutions, successfully!

@subliminal: hope you find the appropriate information in here! Actually, I ran over your issue (blank terminals - no reponse) but this might do the trick! Get back with your results! 
[SIZE="4"]
HowTo set up ATi BigDesktop using fglrx with DIFFERENT screen resolutions in pairmode[/SIZE]

Ziox has made great effort in demonstrating different [dualhead/bigdesktop/mergedFB/twinview capabilities]("http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=221174") of ATi and nVidia cards. Nevertheless, some of us have been struggling to set up BigDesktop with different screen resultions. In my case there are two monitors:
1) an internal LCD screen @ 1400x1050
2) an external monitor @ 1680x1050
The crucial point is setting 'pairmode' correctly in your xorg.conf, forget any 'virtual' or 'mode2' settings...

[SIZE="3"]NOTE 1: pairmode is supported in versions >= 8.28.8 of fglrx!!![/SIZE]

[SIZE="3"]NOTE 2: apply changes in screen resolution where necessary[/SIZE]

Start with having a look at aticonfig's current pairmode capabilites:
```
$ aticonfig --list-pairmode
Get pair modes : 1
 0    1680x1050 + 1680x1050 --> 3360x1050 
```

Although configured in xorg.conf with
```
Section "Device"
       "PairMode" "1400x1050+1680x1050" 
EndSection
```
1400x1050 + 1680x1050 --> 3080x1050 does not show up. 

So, let's backup current xorg.conf as we are going to mess around with it:
```
sudo cp /etc/X11/xorg.conf /etc/X11/xorg.conf.failsafe
```

Then, tell aticonfig to enable additional pairmode capabilites as it does not recognize them via xorg.conf (aticonfig makes some changes to xorg.conf now):
```
$ sudo aticonfig --add-pairmode=1400x1050+1680x1050
Password:
FGLRX_AddPairMode failed when try to add mode : 1400x1050+1680x1050 
Using /etc/X11/xorg.conf
Saved back-up to /etc/X11/xorg.conf.fglrx-0
```

Ignore the error message, test pairmode again!
```
$ aticonfig --list-pairmode
Get pair modes : 2
 0    1680x1050 + 1680x1050 --> 3360x1050 
 1    1400x1050 + 1680x1050 --> 3080x1050 
```

:-D it's there!! aticonfig now interprets xorg.conf correctly... nice!

Now, let's watch our current effort in a separate X session WITHOUT restarting X (no CTRL ALT BACKSPACE). Go to a Terminal, eg tty2 (CTRL ALT F2) and type
```
xinit -- :2
```
This should start an additional X session in tty8 with an xterm session, grey (b+w) background and an "X" as your mouse pointer.
IF IT DOES NOT: in case 'xinit -- :2' resulted in error messages, go back to your current X session CTRL ALT F7 and check xorg.conf and the error messages in Xorg.2.log. To ensure a working xorg.conf next time, restore the original file via 'sudo cp /etc/X11/xorg.conf.failsafe /etc/X11/xorg.conf'.

Now, back to the recently started X session on tty8 - it uses the altered version of xorg.conf.
Let's see if pairmode 1400x1050 + 1680x1050 is supported! In the xterm console do
```
$ xrandr
 SZ:    Pixels          Physical       Refresh
*0   3360 x 1050   ( 948mm x 303mm )  *60  
 1   3080 x 1050   ( 948mm x 303mm )   60  
 2   1400 x 1050   ( 948mm x 303mm )   60  
 3   1680 x 1050   ( 948mm x 303mm )   60  
 4   1280 x 1024   ( 948mm x 303mm )   75   70   60  
 5   1280 x 768    ( 948mm x 303mm )   60  
 6   1152 x 864    ( 948mm x 303mm )   75   70   60  
 7   1024 x 768    ( 948mm x 303mm )   75   72   70   60  
 8   1024 x 480    ( 948mm x 303mm )   60  
 9    848 x 480    ( 948mm x 303mm )   60  
 10   800 x 600    ( 948mm x 303mm )   75   72   70   60   56   47  
 11   720 x 576    ( 948mm x 303mm )   60  
 12   720 x 480    ( 948mm x 303mm )   60  
 13   640 x 480    ( 948mm x 303mm )   75   72   60  
 14   640 x 400    ( 948mm x 303mm )   75   60  
 15   640 x 350    ( 948mm x 303mm )   60  
 16   512 x 384    ( 948mm x 303mm )   60  
 17   400 x 300    ( 948mm x 303mm )   75   60  
 18   320 x 240    ( 948mm x 303mm )   75   60  
 19   320 x 200    ( 948mm x 303mm )   75   60  
Current rotation - normal
Current reflection - none
Rotations possible - normal 
Reflections possible - none

```

The asterix '*' marked setting is your current profile, but we observe a NEW entry: ' 1   3080 x 1050   ( 948mm x 303mm )   60' 

Now, kill tty8 (type 'exit' in xterm) and switch back to tty7 (CTRL ALT F7)

Restart X via CTRL ALT BACKSPACE, you won't observe any changes unless you set your Screen Resolution to '3080x1050' (we know it's there, xrandr told us before)

System > Preferences > Screen Resolution > 3080x1050

Done!

Here is a screenshot of my current desktop and xorg.conf file - using the correct screen resolution on both screens:
[IMG]http://austrian.n3rds.net/uploads/3080x1050.png[/IMG]

[SIZE="3"]NOTE 3: the crucial settings are marked **bold**[/SIZE]

```
# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the /etc/X11/xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man /etc/X11/xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg
#
#   ********************************
#   ** BIG DESKTOP CONFIGURATION  **
#   ********************************
#
#   kEinstein	19-Jan-07


Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier     "Default Layout"
	Screen      0  "aticonfig-Screen[0]" 0 0
	InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
	InputDevice    "stylus" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice    "cursor" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice    "eraser" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice    "Synaptics Touchpad"
EndSection

Section "Files"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/X11/fonts/cyrillic"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi/:unscaled"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/X11/fonts/Type1"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/fonts/X11/misc"
	FontPath     "/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load  "i2c"
	Load  "bitmap"
	Load  "ddc"
	Load  "dri"
	Load  "extmod"
	Load  "freetype"
	Load  "glx"
	Load  "int10"
	Load  "type1"
	Load  "vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Generic Keyboard"
	Driver      "kbd"
	Option	    "CoreKeyboard"
	Option	    "XkbRules" "xorg"
	Option	    "XkbModel" "pc105"
	Option	    "XkbLayout" "de"
	Option	    "XkbVariant" "nodeadkeys"
	Option	    "XkbOptions" "lv3:ralt_switch"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Configured Mouse"
	Driver      "mouse"
	Option	    "CorePointer"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
	Option	    "Protocol" "ExplorerPS/2"
	Option	    "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
	Option	    "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Synaptics Touchpad"
	Driver      "synaptics"
	Option	    "SendCoreEvents" "true"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/psaux"
	Option	    "Protocol" "auto-dev"
	Option	    "HorizScrollDelta" "0"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "stylus"
	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/wacom"
	Option	    "Type" "stylus"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "eraser"
	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/wacom" 
	Option	    "Type" "eraser"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"       
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "cursor"
	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/wacom" 
	Option	    "Type" "cursor"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier   "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
	Option	    "VendorName" "ATI Proprietary Driver"
	Option	    "ModelName" "Generic Autodetecting Monitor"
	Option	    "DPMS" "true"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier  "aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Driver      "fglrx"
	Option	    "DesktopSetup" "horizontal"
	Option	    "VideoOverlay" "on"
	Option	    "OpenGLOverlay" "off"
	Option	    "EnablePrivateBackZ" "yes"
	Option	    "HSync2" "65"
	Option	    "VRefresh2" "60"
	**Option	    "PairModes" "1400x1050+1680x1050"**
	BusID       "PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier "aticonfig-Screen[0]"
	Device     "aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Monitor    "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
	DefaultDepth     24
	SubSection "Display"
		Viewport   0 0
		Depth     24
		**Modes    "1400x1050" "1680x1050"**
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "DRI"
	Mode         0666
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
	Option	    "Composite" "Disable"
EndSection
```

with fglrx enabled:
```
$ fglrxinfo
display: :0.0  screen: 0
OpenGL vendor string: ATI Technologies Inc.
OpenGL renderer string: MOBILITY RADEON X300 Generic
OpenGL version string: 2.0.6011 (8.28.8)
```

Finally, I hope this will help you in solving your issues!

Should I delete the following section from my xorg.conf file, which is just prior to the ATI configured section?

```
Section "Device"
	Identifier  "Generic Video Card"
	Driver      "vesa"
	BusID       "PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection
```

Thanks,
CH

### Post by tailStrike on 2008-02-16
Thanks for the good Info Ziox.  I now have both displays kicking.  Couple of things to note:

Fist of all, I am running Kubuntu 6.06 and followed these instructions to install the driver ( 8.25.18 ) on an otherwise Vinilla install:
[http://wiki.cchtml.com/index.php/Ubuntu_Dapper_Installation_Guide#Method_1:_Installing_Dapper.27s_Included_Driver_.288.25.18.29](http://wiki.cchtml.com/index.php/Ubuntu_Dapper_Installation_Guide#Method_1:_Installing_Dapper.27s_Included_Driver_.288.25.18.29)

- In step 2, it was necessary for me to replace the '--initial' argument with '--initial=dual-head' and include the '--desktop-setup=horizontal' argument.

This adds the necessary info to the "ServerLayout" section, which seems to be responsible for the mouse boundary as before doing this, I had a working 2nd display with nothing but a blue background image that could not be accessed with the mouse.

- Next, note that the '--add-pairmode=Width0xHeight0+Width1xHeight1' is not valid in my version of thet driver.  It was apparently changed at some point, as for me the argument was '--mode2'.  So, I used '--mode2=1280x1024,1280x1024'

So, the steps for the version 8.25.18 driver are:

2.    sudo aticonfig --initial=dual-head --desktop-setup=horizontal --overlay-type=Xv

3.    sudo aticonfig --sync-vsync=on --mode2=Width0xHeight0+Width1xHeight1

Noting that Width0, Height0, Width1, and Height1 are to be replaced with the horizontal and vertical resolutions of each monitor, respectively.

Hope this helps!

---

### Post by Arituay on 2008-02-28
> **tailStrike said:**
> 
- In step 2, it was necessary for me to replace the '--initial' argument with '--initial=dual-head' and include the '--desktop-setup=horizontal' argument.
<snip>

- Next, note that the **'--add-pairmode=Width0xHeight0+Width1xHeight1' is NOT valid** in my version of thet driver.  It was apparently changed at some point, as for me the argument was '--mode2'.  So, I used '--mode2=1280x1024,1280x1024'


Emphasis above mine.

Thank you tailStrike!  This worked for me, I was beginning to think it would never work again.  I had Big Desktop working in Feisty (I had used --add=pairmode... back then) then upgrading to Gutsy broke it.  I am guessing Gutsy came with a newer version of fglrx in which the --add-pairmode command is broken.  One thing to note is that my xorg.conf had a line which contained pairmode from when I was messing around with it and it didn't work.  When I deleted that line it worked for me upon reboot.

Note that the --initial=dual-head won't get you Big Desktop by my understanding.  If you want Big Desktop use 'aticonfig --initial' instead.

One additional step is vital to getting Big Desktop to work after  you have used the aticonfig commands as outlined by tailStrike and myself above and verified that your xorg.conf looks right (see mine below).  You must reboot then go to System>Preferences>Screen Resolution and change your resolution to the largest size (mine is 2560x1024) and click the check box to make that the default setting.

Here is my working Big Desktop xorg.conf file.  The items in bold are the keys to getting Big Desktop to work, so make sure yours has them.  Note that I have fglrx driver version 8.37.6, so I don't know how this works on different versions.

```
# xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by failsafeDexconf, using
# values from the debconf database and some overrides to use vesa mode.
#
# You should use dexconf or another such tool for creating a "real" xorg.conf
# For example:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier     "Default Layout"
**	Screen      0  "aticonfig-Screen[0]" 0 0**
	InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load  "glx"
	Load  "GLcore"
	Load  "v4l"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Generic Keyboard"
	Driver      "kbd"
	Option	    "CoreKeyboard"
	Option	    "XkbRules" "xorg"
	Option	    "XkbModel" "pc105"
	Option	    "XkbLayout" "us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Configured Mouse"
	Driver      "mouse"
	Option	    "CorePointer"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
	Option	    "Protocol" "ImPS/2"
	Option	    "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
	Option	    "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
**	Identifier   "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"**
	Option	    "VendorName" "ATI Proprietary Driver"
	Option	    "ModelName" "Generic Autodetecting Monitor"
	Option	    "DPMS" "true"
EndSection

Section "Device"
[B]	Identifier  "aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Driver      "fglrx"
	Option	    "DesktopSetup" "horizontal"[/B]
	Option	    "VideoOverlay" "on"
	Option	    "OpenGLOverlay" "off"
	Option	    "EnablePrivateBackZ" "yes"
	Option	    "HSync2" "65"
	Option	    "VRefresh2" "60"
**	Option	    "Mode2" "1280x1024,1280x1024"**
	BusID       "PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	[B]Identifier "aticonfig-Screen[0]"
	Device     "aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Monitor    "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"[/B]
	DefaultDepth     24
	SubSection "Display"
**		Virtual   1280 1024**
		Depth     24
		Modes    "1280x1024@60" "1280x960@75" "1280x960@60" "1400x1050@60" "1280x1024@75" "1600x1200@60" "1152x864@75" "1024x768@60" "1024x768@70" "1024x768@75" "832x624@75" "800x600@60" "800x600@75" "800x600@72" "800x600@56" "640x480@75" "640x480@72" "640x480@60"
	EndSubSection
EndSection

[B]Section "DRI"
	Mode         0666
EndSection[/B]

[B]Section "Extensions"
	Option	    "Composite" "Disable"
EndSection

Section "ServerFlags"
        Option  "AIGLX" "off"
EndSection[/B]

```

I hope that helps someone.

### Post by tekDragon on 2008-03-07
OK... After a ridiculously long trial and error session I've managed to get mine working. I had to take a few shots in the dark but it worked in the end so I thought I'd share how I did it.

I have a laptop with a Mobility radeon X700
Monitor @ 1280x800
Monitor @ 1680x1050
7.10
Latest binary driver installed with Envy


I think the source of the most problems is that the primary monitor is smaller than the secondary (but what the heck do I know).

Following the 1st method from the first post worked to get me to a shared 1280x800 times two desktop but no matter what I tried I wasnt able to get the paired mode option for 1280x800+1680+1050 to stick.

I followed the directions from post #29 but it just wouldnt work... So more and more reading later...  here are the key commands I followed.

#1 ----> Make a backup of your xorg.conf

Then... as per 1st post
```
sudo aticonfig --desktop-setup=horizontal --sync-vsync=on --add-pairmode=Width0xHeight0+Width1xHeight1

```

Then I disregarded the results of the query monitor command
```
sudo aticonfig --enable-monitor=auto,auto
```
```
sudo aticonfig --force-monitor=auto,auto
```

Restart X with ctrl+alt+backspace
I now had the 1680x1050 + 1680x1050 --> 3360x1050 pairmode option open...  but that's too big.

I followed the instructions from post #29 to include the option I needed for the desktop to be just right.

```
sudo aticonfig --add-pairmode=1280x800+1680x1050
```

Double check with xrander that the new option is there, and I was able to pick the correct resolution right away.

Hope this helps someone.

---

### Post by Arituay on 2008-03-07
> **Amendment44 said:**
> Im trying to get these dual monitors working and for some reason I just cant get out of clone mode. 

im useing a sapphire x1550 radeon 512m card
gutsy

When i try the command

matt@matt-desktop:~$ aticonfig --initial=dual-head --screen-layout=above

I get the following

Found fglrx primary device section
Found fglrx secondary device section
Using /etc/X11/xorg.conf
Saved back-up to /etc/X11/xorg.conf.fglrx-9
aticonfig: Writing to '/etc/X11/xorg.conf' failed. Bad file descriptor.



In order to execute an aticonfig command you need to use sudo since it edits the xorg.conf file, so the command you are trying would need to be:

matt@matt-desktop:~$ sudo aticonfig --initial=dual-head --screen-layout=above

# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Files"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"
	Driver		"kbd"
	Option		"CoreKeyboard"
	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc105"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"mouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/mice"
	Option		"Protocol"	"ImPS/2"
	Option		"ZAxisMapping"	"4 5"
	Option		"Emulate3Buttons"	"true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Identifier	"stylus"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"	"stylus"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Identifier	"eraser"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"	"eraser"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Identifier	"cursor"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"	"cursor"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"ATI Technologies Inc ATI Default Card"
	Driver		"fglrx"
	Busid		"PCI:1:0:0"
Option "VideoOverlay" "on"
Option "OpenGLOverlay" "off"
Option "DesktopSetup" "horizontal"
Option "Mode2" "1280x1024" #Resolution for second monitor
Option "DesktopSetup" "LVDS,AUTO" #the types of monitors that is connected LVDS = LCD, CRT, AUTO
Option "EnablePrivateBackZ" "yes" #Enable 3d support <= May Not Work
Option "HSync2" "65" #This sets the horizontal sync for the secondary display.
Option "VRefresh2" "60" #This sets the refresh rate of the secondary display.
BusID "PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"SyncMaster"
	Option		"DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Device		"ATI Technologies Inc ATI Default Card"
	Monitor		"SyncMaster"
	Defaultdepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Modes		"1280x1024"	"1280x960"	"1152x864"	"1024x768"	"832x624"	"800x600"	"720x400"	"640x480"
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
  screen "Default Screen"
	Inputdevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	Inputdevice	"Configured Mouse"
	
	# Uncomment if you have a wacom tablet
	#	InputDevice     "stylus"	"SendCoreEvents"
	#	InputDevice     "cursor"	"SendCoreEvents"
	#	InputDevice     "eraser"	"SendCoreEvents"
EndSection
Section "Module"
	Load		"glx"
EndSection
```

### Post by earthmeLon on 2008-04-29
I have installed Ubuntu on many computers with dual monitors.  The following has worked for me everytime:

Revert xorg.conf back to original
Install envy: [http://albertomilone.com/nvidia_scripts1.html](http://albertomilone.com/nvidia_scripts1.html)
Run envy, install drivers.
When envy asks if you want it to configure your xorg.conf, say yes.
Reboot.


Seems really simple, but I hope it works out for you, too :D

---

### Post by 3.1416 on 2008-04-30
After more than a few unsuccessful attempts, these instructions worked perfectly for me. I have a similar setup: ATI X1650 (with drivers from EnvyNG), Ubuntu 8.04, monitors at 1680x1050 and 1280x1024. The "sudo aticonfig --enable-monitor=auto,auto" command left the displays in a weird state where all I could see was the last line I had typed in the terminal, but restarting X cleared that up. I was then able to select 3360x1050 in Screen Resolution, and after the final "aticonfig --add-pairmode=1680x1050+1280x1024" and restarting X again I could get the correct 2960x1050. 

Thank you very much; this was the last hardware obstacle before starting the process of switching my primary desktop from a Power Mac G5 to a quad core Linux box. *So* much faster :)

> **tekDragon said:**
> OK... After a ridiculously long trial and error session I've managed to get mine working. I had to take a few shots in the dark but it worked in the end so I thought I'd share how I did it.

I have a laptop with a Mobility radeon X700
Monitor @ 1280x800
Monitor @ 1680x1050
7.10
Latest binary driver installed with Envy


I think the source of the most problems is that the primary monitor is smaller than the secondary (but what the heck do I know).

Following the 1st method from the first post worked to get me to a shared 1280x800 times two desktop but no matter what I tried I wasnt able to get the paired mode option for 1280x800+1680+1050 to stick.

I followed the directions from post #29 but it just wouldnt work... So more and more reading later...  here are the key commands I followed.

#1 ----> Make a backup of your xorg.conf

Then... as per 1st post
```
sudo aticonfig --desktop-setup=horizontal --sync-vsync=on --add-pairmode=Width0xHeight0+Width1xHeight1

```

Then I disregarded the results of the query monitor command
```
sudo aticonfig --enable-monitor=auto,auto
```
```
sudo aticonfig --force-monitor=auto,auto
```

Restart X with ctrl+alt+backspace
I now had the 1680x1050 + 1680x1050 --> 3360x1050 pairmode option open...  but that's too big.

I followed the instructions from post #29 to include the option I needed for the desktop to be just right.

```
sudo aticonfig --add-pairmode=1280x800+1680x1050
```

Double check with xrander that the new option is there, and I was able to pick the correct resolution right away.

Hope this helps someone.

---

### Post by vindi on 2008-04-30
Finally !!  **3.1416** Thank you.. and thanks to **earthmeLon** for info about Envy -- I thought that was only for nVidia cards.

I restored my orginal xorg.conf and installed the envy drivers.  This part was strange because my xorg.conf after the envy install was very generic looking.  Yet I still had my big desktop stuck in 1024 on both monitors like before the envy driver.  I tried CTRL-ALT-BACKSPACE and a reboot too..  I removed ALL other xorg.confs too including failsafes and still had Big Desktop working.  Im very confused by this.. is there another location these settings are stored?  anyway.. 

I followed 3.1416's steps and adjusted my resolutions and it works.. !

Im even getting 85Hz (max rate for the resolution on my crt -- awesome)  I cant stand that 60Hz flicker.

Thanks again.. still a bit confused as to why X11 was running my borked big desktop setting still even though I restored the xorg.conf..  ?

### Post by quizzelsnatch on 2008-05-01
So, just wanted to say, I was trying this out for like 2 hours and I couldn't figure out what was going on. I had it spanned before I logged in, but as soon as I logged in it would go to clone and I was like WTF? That makes no sense!
Long story short... I didn't change my screen resolution from 1680x1050 to 3360x1050. As soon as I did that it fixed it all.

Side note: with big desktop, when I click on my desktop/background a few times in a row it restarts X and brings me back to the login screen, anybody know anything about that?

### Post by in_rainbow on 2008-05-02
Thanks for the guide.

I spent 2 days trying to get this working in Kubuntu Hardy ](*,)

I have an ATI Radeon X1300, My main monitor is 1280x1024 and my second monitor is to the right and is 1024x768.

I found that I couldn't get the 64bit drivers installed and reverted to 32bit.

I found that the guide using aticonfig cause me alot of problems for some reason, couldn't get the proper resolution out of my main monitor. It eventually got it working\\:D/ by just copying in the following section into my xorg.conf (as mentioned at the bottom of the first post.)

```
Option "DesktopSetup"  "horizontal" #Enable Big Desktop
Option "Mode2"         "1280x1024" #Resolution for second monitor
Option "DesktopSetup" "LVDS,AUTO" #the types of monitors that is connected LVDS = LCD, CRT, AUTO
Option "EnablePrivateBackZ" "yes" #Enable 3d support <= May Not Work
Option "HSync2" "65" #This sets the horizontal sync for the secondary display. 
Option "VRefresh2" "60" #This sets the refresh rate of the secondary display.
```

So for those that are having trouble, here's my working xorg.conf, hope it helps. Also *grandr* is a handy tool to install to check and change your current resolution, it's basically a GUI for the command line xrandr

```

# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier     "Default Layout"
	Screen      0  "aticonfig-Screen[0]" 0 0
EndSection

Section "Files"
EndSection

Section "Module"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Generic Keyboard"
	Driver      "kbd"
	Option	    "XkbRules" "xorg"
	Option	    "XkbModel" "pc105"
	Option	    "XkbLayout" "ie"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Configured Mouse"
	Driver      "mouse"
	Option	    "CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier   "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
	Option	    "VendorName" "ATI Proprietary Driver"
	Option	    "ModelName" "Generic Autodetecting Monitor"
	Option	    "DPMS" "true"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier  "aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Driver      "fglrx"
	Option "DesktopSetup"  "horizontal" #Enable Big Desktop
	Option "Mode2"         "1280x1024" #Resolution for second monitor
	Option "DesktopSetup" "LVDS,AUTO" #the types of monitors that is connected LVDS = LCD, CRT, AUTO
	Option "EnablePrivateBackZ" "yes" #Enable 3d support <= May Not Work
	Option "HSync2" "65" #This sets the horizontal sync for the secondary display. 
	Option "VRefresh2" "60" #This sets the refresh rate of the secondary display.
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier "aticonfig-Screen[0]"
	Device     "aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Monitor    "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
	DefaultDepth     24
	SubSection "Display"
		Viewport   0 0
		Depth     24
	EndSubSection
EndSection

```

### Post by dvd23 on 2008-05-02
Just to say that I followed the instructions of 3.1416,
and I think I found my problem :

the screen in 1680x1050 is working perfect, but when I try to --add-pairmode=1680x1050+1280x1024 it's not working : FGLRX_AddPairMode failed when try to add mode : 1680x1050+1280x1024

So i tried with a lower resolution :
--add-pairmode=1680x1050+1024x768 and that's perfectly working.
No more troubles, the X server doesn't restart when I click on the desktop...

But I'd like to have the second screen in 1280x1024...
I'll continue searching, 
and thanks :)

### Post by Derviansoul on 2008-05-06
Hello everyone

i've being playing around with bigdesktop for a while and before i had two screens with different maximum resolutions, aparently bigdesktop doesn't like  it, i found a work around in this thread and i add the virtual command to my primary screen with my total resolution from both screens, ex: 1440x900+1280x1024 == virtual 2720 1024, this fixed my problem but now the issues, using this command for gnome workspace applet each of my virtual desktops had the size of 2720x2 and compiz wouldn't work, small dialogs that on a normal bigdesktop configuration show up on the center right between both screens, with this setup they would show up half hidden half showed up on the right of the second screen, my conclusion is that for gnome the total size of each of my desktops was those 5440, i search everywhere for a solution for this, but i couldn't find it though, i end up giving up and do something that i wanted to do for along time, replacing the second screen! My previous configuration though worked fine no crashed or any problems!! Now i've got bigdesktop working with the same resolutions because i replaced the screen!!
but if anyone wants to try the solution that i used with the virtual command i leave the xorg.conf here everything worked but when it comes to the compiz i couldn't find a solution for it.


```




# xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "ServerLayout"

	# Uncomment if you have a wacom tablet
	#	InputDevice     "stylus"	"SendCoreEvents"
	#	InputDevice     "cursor"	"SendCoreEvents"
	#	InputDevice     "eraser"	"SendCoreEvents"
	Identifier     "Default Layout"
	Screen      0  "aticonfig-Screen[0]" 0 0
	InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
EndSection

Section "Files"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Generic Keyboard"
	Driver      "kbd"
	Option	    "CoreKeyboard"
	Option	    "XkbRules" "xorg"
	Option	    "XkbModel" "pc105"
	Option	    "XkbLayout" "gb"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Configured Mouse"
	Driver      "mouse"
	Option	    "CorePointer"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
	Option	    "Protocol" "ExplorerPS/2"
	Option	    "Buttons" "10"
	Option	    "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
	Option	    "ButtonMapping" "1 2 3 6 7"
	#       Option "Protocol" "ImPS/2"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "stylus"
	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
	Option	    "Type" "stylus"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "eraser"
	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
	Option	    "Type" "eraser"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "cursor"
	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
	Option	    "Type" "cursor"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection


Section "Device"
	Identifier  "aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Driver      "fglrx"
	BoardName   "ati"
	Option	    "HSync2" "65.29"#The horizontal sync for the secondary display.
	Option	    "VRefresh2" "59.954"#The refresh rate of the secondary display.
	#Option	    "DesktopSetup" "horizontal"
	Option	    "OverlayOnCRTC2" "1"
	Option	    "XAANoOffscreenPixmaps" "1"
	#Option	    "TexturedVideo" "1"
        #Option          "TexturedVideoSync"     "1"
	#Option	    "VideoOverlay" "off"
	#Option	    "OpenGLOverlay" "off"
	#Option	    "Textured2D" "on"
	#Option	    "TexturedXrender" "on"
	Option	    "UseFastTLS" "0" #->Enable/disable fast thread local storage. Disable this option when virtual machines or WineX fail to work properly.
	Option	    "BackingStore" "on" #->Helps alot. See Note 3...
	Option	    "PairModes" "1440x900+1280x1024"
	BusID       "PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Screen" #primary screen section.
	Identifier "aticonfig-Screen[0]"
	Device     "aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Monitor    "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
	DefaultDepth     24
	SubSection "Display"
		Virtual   2720 1024 
		Depth     24
		Modes    "1440x900@75" "1280x1024@75" #
          EndSubSection

EndSection

Section "DRI"
	Mode         0666
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
	Option	    "Composite" "Enable"
EndSection

```

Hope this helps for the ones trying to find a solution for the dual monitor setup.
regards

---

# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by failsafeDexconf, using

	Monitor    "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
	DefaultDepth     24
	SubSection "Display"
		Viewport   0 0
		Depth     24
	EndSubSection
EndSection

hi all,
i've got a similar issue as described above on an ibm thinkpad t43p with kubuntu 8.04 and an ibm 19" flat panel, connected to the vga port of the docking station ... i've followed the instructions of this thread with a few minor modifications along the way. basically what i want is to have a big desktop with my laptop display as the primary screen at its maximum resolution of 1400x1050 and use the flat panel as the secondary screen at a 1280x1024. after plenty of trial and error i got my x server to accept the config, but my lcd flat panel just doesn't display anything, goes into power saving mode and that's it. although it would seem that the x server is running in the big desktop mode - on the login screen i can see the cursor first appearing by the right side of my laptop screen, as if in the middle of the two screens (same as i had on my other installation before this one) and once kde loads i can "move" the cursor and windows onto the extended desktop of the second screen - i just don't see anything on the flat panel as if there's no signal coming from the laptop?!
any help would be appreciated in getting this setup running, i'd really like to have the benefit of running two screens with different resolutions on them. thanks!

here's what my laptop's config looks like:
xorg.conf:
```

	Option	    "OpenGLOverlay" "off"

### Post by estebistec on 2008-05-21
Hello,

I have two monitors that are the same model, Dell 2208wfp's. At one point one of them went bad and had to be replaced (again, with the same model). From that point on, the second monitor is not taking on the higher resolution that they are both configured for. They should both be 1680x1050, and the resolution setting reports 3360x1050. However the second monitor is clearly much coarser than that, probably 1024x768, but it's hard to tell because the system is reporting what I want to see. So, it's as if the display is refusing to use the settings for the second monitor, but not reporting that in any way.

Here are the relevant sections from my xorg.conf. Thanks ahead of time for any help that might be offered.

	Identifier   "DELL 2208WFP"
	VendorName   "Generic LCD Display"

### Post by markbuntu on 2008-05-21
This is what I have found after fooling around with the ATI restricted driver and the catalyst control center and the 386i and amd64 distros for about a week.

You can run the Catalyst Control Center once only. After that it does not make any changes reliably after the first time you run it. If you want to change your setup you must reinstall fglrx, reboot with CTRL ALT backspace and reconfigure with catalyst and reboot again. It is fairly quick and painless. The repositories now have the latest ATI driver and have just updated the Open Gl part of it yesterday so there is no reason to get it anywhere else and no reason to install or reinstall it with anything but Synaptic.

If you have two different size monitors you should make the larger one display 1. catalyst does not seem to be able to configure big desktop to the proper size if the smaller one is display 1. You should also check the box "Scale image to full panel size" on the display 2

For now, compiz is unable to use direct rendering with the ATI driver so if you do not have at least 1.5GB ram it will crash or run real slow. You will have fewer problems with the x86_amd64 kernel. If you have a 64 bit processor I reccomend you get the 64 bit Kernel. On 386i, compiz will max out your cpu continuously and cause it to overheat and crash.

If you do not have 
fglrx version 8.47.3 and
Catalyst Control Center Version 1.8 and
Open GL version 2.1.7412 Release
you should get them with update manager

If you have an ATI card supported by the open source drivers you should check out the latest update as they have added 2D acceleration support to many older cards recently.

HP 1330n Media Center (modified)
ASUS A8AE-LE motherboard (OEM)
AMD Athlon64 3800+ 2.9GHZ 939 socket
3GB 3200 DDR RAM
ATI Radeon Express 200M (disabled in bios)
ATI HD3650 1GB DDR2  PCI Express
AC97 on board sound
Realtek 810L on board 10/100 ethernet

SOYO 24 inch LCD wide screen monitor 1920x 1200
Samsung 930B 19 inch LCD 1280x1024


Ubuntu 8.04 "Hardy Heron" - Release amd64
Kernel Linux 2.6.24-16-generic
ATI Proprietary Linux Driver Version Identifier:8.47.3
Catalyst Control Center Version 1.8
Open GL version 2.1.7412 Release

Big Desktop, Compiz, Emerald

---

### Post by tadrinth on 2008-05-29
I've looked through this thread and I haven't found anything that looks like the issues I'm running into. 

I have two different 1280x1024 LCD monitors hooked up to a Radeon x1650.  One monitor is a Neovo F-419, the other is an Emachines E17T4.  Under XP, both monitors must be at their max resolution and 75 Hz, or the primary monitor constantly flickers between black and normal every 2s.  

I have a fresh install of Hardy Heron and I'm using the latest fglrx from ATI's website.  My desired setup is a 2560x1024 Big Desktop with both screens at 75 Hz, though as long as the flicker issue isn't coming up I'd be fine with 60 Hz.  I can run a 2048x768 Big Desktop and it "just works" with no tweaking needed, specifying with a Pairmode option.  

First problem: fglrx does not detect either monitor's 1280x1024 resolution, according to /var/log/Xorg.0.log  

I corrected this for the first monitor by adding a modeling in my xorg.conf:
ModeLine     "1280x1024" 156.4 1280 1312 1904 1936 1024 1043 1056 1076

This lets me use Gnome's screen resolution GUI to run the main monitor at the right resolution.  

Second problem:
Regardless of whether I use pairmodes, Mode2, or any other options, I cannot get the second monitor to properly display at 1280x1024.  Instead, it displays 1024x768 pixels of a 2560x1024 desktop.  Its still at the wrong resolution, but I have extra desktop offscreen that appears to match the correct resolution.  Under some conditions (I've been tweaking my xorg.conf for multiple weekends) I can get the second monitor to 'pan' around the extra undisplayed space. 

This configuration also triggers the flicker issue.  Its listed in xranr as a 60 Hz refresh rate, even though the modeline is for 75Hz and I've tried specifying 75Hz using VRefresh2.  

I would like to use a modeline to specify the resolution of the second monitor, but I can't figure out how to do that.  If I can correct fglrx to autodetect the proper resolution, that would work too.  

Also, my display defaults to 1280x1024 cloned, not the 2560x1024 desired.  I have to switch using the gnome GUI, which always results in the same result regardless of tweaks to the xorg.conf.  Is there a way to set up the pairmode as preferred? How are the refresh rates for the two monitors specified using pairmode?

```

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier   "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
	ModeLine     "1280x1024" 156.4 1280 1312 1904 1936 1024 1043 1056 1076
	Option	    "VendorName" "ATI Proprietary Driver"
	Option	    "ModelName" "Neovo"
	Option	    "DPMS" "true"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier  "aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Driver      "fglrx"
	Option	    "DesktopSetup" "horizontal"
	Option	    "Capabilities" "0x00000800"
	#Option	    "PairModes" "0x0+0x0,1280x1024+1280x1024"
	Option	    "Mode2" "1280x1024"
	Option	    "ForceMonitors" "tmds1,crt2"
	Option 	    "DesktopSetup" "LVDS,LVDS"
	Option 	     "HSync2" "65" 
	Option 	     "VRefresh2" "60" 
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier "aticonfig-Screen[0]"
	Device     "aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Monitor    "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
	DefaultDepth     24
	SubSection "Display"

		#Virtual	  2560 1024
		Viewport   0 0
		Depth     24
		Modes    "1280x1024"
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
	Option	    "Composite" "Disable"
EndSection
```

### Post by gfxguy on 2008-05-30
Ok, I've run through both the MergedFB and BigDesktop instructions, and can't quite get what I want.

It's on a laptop, with Radeon 200M card; the laptop display is 1280x800, the external monitor is 1280x1024.

Using the described methods, I can only get 1024x768 showing up on the external monitor.

The xorg.conf below is completely roll-your-own, and it's the best I've been able to get so far.  I'd appreciate any suggestions on how to get a big desktop, full resolution on the external monitor.

The configuration below is the only way I've been able to get full resolution on the external monitor, but it creates two desktops, and I can't drag and drop between the two, or move application from one to another; it's almost like being logged into two machines.  Worst, if I'm working on one display for too long, the screen saver kicks in on the other and just screws everything up... I can't even load the screensaver control panel.

```

# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"
	Driver		"kbd"
	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc105"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"mouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Synaptics Touchpad"
	Driver		"synaptics"
	Option		"SendCoreEvents"	"true"
	Option		"Device"		"/dev/psaux"
	Option		"Protocol"		"auto-dev"
	Option		"HorizEdgeScroll"	"0"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Card0"
#	VendorName	"ATI"
#	BoardName	"Radeon X200 (Laptop)"
	Driver		"fglrx"
	BusID		"PCI:1:5:0"
	Screen		0
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Card1"
#	VendorName	"ATI"
#	BoardName	"Radeon X200 (External)"
	Driver		"fglrx"
	BusID		"PCI:1:5:0"
	Screen		1
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"LaptopMonitor"
	VendorName	"Toshiba"
	ModelName	"Built In 1280x800"
EndSection

Section  "Monitor"
	Identifier	"ExternalMonitor"
	VendorName	"Samsung"
	ModelName	"713v"
	HorizSync	30.0 - 81.0
	VertRefresh	56.0 - 75.0
	Option		"DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Screen0"
	Device		"Card0"
	Monitor		"LaptopMonitor"
	DefaultDepth     24
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth	24
		Modes	"1280x800"
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Screen1"
	Device		"Card1"
	Monitor		"ExternalMonitor"
	DefaultDepth     24
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth     24
		Modes    "1280x1024"
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
	Screen		0 "Screen0"
	InputDevice	"Synaptics Touchpad"
	InputDevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice	"Configured Mouse"
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Multihead Layout"
	Screen		0 "Screen0"
	Screen		1 "Screen1" RightOf "Screen0"
	InputDevice	"Synaptics Touchpad"
	InputDevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice	"Configured Mouse"
EndSection

Section "ServerFlags"
	Option		"DefaultServerLayout" "Multihead Layout"
EndSection

```

I'd also like some education on how this is supposed to work - everyone else seems to only have one device, one monitor, one screen... I can't run something like this:
```
aticonfig --resolution=1,1280x1024
```
Without errors unless I actually have a second screen defined.  So logically speaking, I can understand if I'm supposed to have one large screen running on one device, but at least with two monitors, I'd assume...

TIA.
